comments.explanation: The paper emphasizes real-time performance but omits FLOPs and parameter count for the final AASeg model in the primary comparison tables (Table 1 and 2). While Table 6 includes parameters for ablation variants, the main result lacks these metrics. This prevents fair comparison with models like STDC-Seg or BiSeNetV2, which report both FPS and FLOPs. The 'real-time' claim hinges on efficiency, and without standardized cost metrics, the speed-accuracy trade-off cannot be fairly evaluated. The authors should compute and report FLOPs and parameters for AASeg at 512×1024 resolution and include them in Table 1.

comments.title: No justification for backbone-free design
comments.quote: Our network dosen’t use any backbone to extract features from the input image unlike many previous architectures.
comments.explanation: The paper states that AASeg uses no backbone, which is highly unusual and potentially detrimental. Most real-time models use lightweight backbones (e.g., MobileNet, ResNet-18, STDC) to extract hierarchical features efficiently. Designing a network from scratch without a proven backbone typically results in inferior feature learning. The paper provides no ablation or justification for this choice, nor does it compare against a backbone-equipped variant. This raises concerns about whether the results stem from the attention design or an underconstrained architecture. The authors should either add a comparison to a standard backbone or provide a controlled experiment showing that their design matches or exceeds backbone-based models at similar complexity.

comments.title: Inconsistent resolution undermines FPS comparisons
comments.quote:
|  Model | Resolution | Backbone | mIoU val(%) | mIoU test(%) | FPS  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  DFANet B | 1024 × 1024 | Xception B | - | 67.1 | 120  |
|  DFANet A | 1024 × 1024 | Xception A | - | 71.3 | 100  |
|  BiSeNetV1 | 768 × 1536 | Xception39 | 69.0 | 68.4 | 105.8  |
comments.explanation: Table 1 compares FPS across models using different input resolutions—1024×1024, 768×1536, and 512×1024—without normalization. Since inference speed scales inversely with resolution, direct FPS comparison is misleading. For example, AASeg achieves 202.7 FPS at 512×1024, but this is not meaningfully faster than BiSeNetV2 at 156 FPS on the same resolution. The lack of FLOPs exacerbates this issue. To enable fair comparison, the authors should either report all speeds at a standard resolution or include FLOPs to contextualize efficiency.

comments.title: Overstates computational efficiency of attention modules
comments.quote: We use Spatial Attention(SA) and Channel Attention(CA) modules to enhance features of objects without adding extra computational cost.
comments.explanation: The claim that SA and CA modules enhance features 'without adding extra computational cost' is factually incorrect. Attention mechanisms inherently involve additional operations—global average pooling, 1×1 convolutions, ReLU, sigmoid—which increase FLOPs and latency. Even if the overhead is small, it is not zero. The paper does not provide evidence that these modules are cost-neutral, and such a claim contradicts standard understanding in efficient network design. This overstatement undermines the credibility of the efficiency claims, especially given the omission of FLOPs in main comparisons. The sentence should be revised to 'with minimal additional computational cost' to reflect reality.

comments.title: Contradictory dilated convolution design description
comments.quote: The dilated convolution layer input at every stage is formed by concatenating the input feature map with the outputs from previous convolutions. At the final step, the outputs from the three dilated convolutions are concatenated with the input feature map.
comments.explanation: The description presents a logical contradiction. It states that each dilated convolution stage takes as input the concatenation of the original feature map and outputs from previous convolutions—implying a sequential, recursive architecture. Yet it also says the final output is formed by concatenating the three dilated outputs with the input, which suggests parallel processing. These two interpretations are incompatible. Moreover, no clarification is given about channel dimensions or transformations before fusion. The authors must clarify whether the dilated convolutions are applied in parallel or sequentially and revise the text accordingly.

comments.title: Missing specification of convolutional branch channels in MSC
comments.quote: The feature map after passing through convolutional block is split to three different parts with 1$\times$1 convolution, 3$\times$3 convolution and 5$\times$5 convolution respectively. The individual feature maps are then fused together. The output fused feature map is first convolved with a $1\times 1$ convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256.
comments.explanation: The text fails to specify the number of output channels for each of the three convolutional branches (1×1, 3×3, 5×5). Without this, the total dimension after concatenation cannot be 2048. For instance, if each branch outputs 512 channels, the concatenated result would be 1536. The value 2048 must either be derived from explicit per-branch channel counts or replaced with a symbolic placeholder. The current presentation risks misleading implementation and undermines reproducibility. The authors should specify the output channels for each branch or define the total dimension symbolically.

comments.title: No handling of spatial misalignment in concatenation
comments.quote: At the final step, the outputs from the three dilated convolutions are concatenated with the input feature map.
comments.explanation: The concatenation assumes spatial alignment (same H×W dimensions), but dilated convolutions with rates 3, 6, and 12 can alter spatial dimensions depending on kernel size, padding, and stride. Without explicit mention of padding strategies (e.g., 'same' padding), the feature maps may not align spatially, making concatenation invalid. This is a critical implementation gap, especially in real-time segmentation where fixed-resolution processing is typical. The authors should specify that all dilated convolutions use dilation-aware padding to preserve spatial dimensions.

comments.title: Missing bottleneck in spatial attention design
comments.quote: f_{SA}(x)=f_{sigmoid}\left(W_{2}\left(f_{ReLU}\left(W_{1}(x)\right)\right)\right)$ (2)
comments.explanation: The spatial attention module applies two 1×1 convolutions without specifying the intermediate channel dimension. If the input has C channels and the intermediate layer also has C channels, the parameter count becomes C² + C, which for C=512 exceeds 260K parameters—excessive for real-time operation. Efficient designs like CBAM use a bottleneck (C/r) to reduce cost. The paper neither specifies the intermediate dimension nor justifies omitting a bottleneck, undermining claims of efficiency. The authors should define W₁: C → C/r and W₂: C/r → 1, and clarify the reduction ratio r.

comments.title: Misleading uniform treatment of MSC in AASeg
comments.quote:
ASeg}=\left(\left(f_{SA}\left(x_{concat}\right)\otimes x_{concat}\right)\oplus\left(f_{CA}\left(x_{concat}\right)\otimes x_{concat}\right)\oplus\left(f_{MSC}\left(x_{concat}\right)\otimes x_{concat}\right)\right)$ (5)

<!--
comments.explanation: Equation (5) applies the same structure f(·) ⊗ x_concat to f_MSC as to f_SA and f_CA, implying MSC produces a multiplicative attention map. However, MSC is described as a multi-scale context module, which typically transforms features via convolutions at different scales, not as a gating mechanism. Applying f_MSC(x_concat) ⊗ x_concat suggests it outputs attention weights, which is not standard. If f_MSC outputs transformed features, the operation should be f_MSC(x_concat) alone. The authors should either redefine f_MSC to output attention weights or revise Equation (5) to treat MSC as a transformation rather than a modulator.

comments.title: Inconsistent feature count from two branches
comments.quote: where $\oplus$ represents the concatenation operator and $x_{1}$, $x_{2}$ and $x_{3}$ represents the features of the two branches.
comments.explanation: The text states that three feature maps x₁, x₂, x₃ originate from 'the two branches', which is logically inconsistent. A two-branch architecture cannot produce three distinct features without further explanation. The authors must either revise the number of branches to three or clarify how the two branches generate three outputs (e.g., one branch producing two feature maps). As written, the description misaligns with standard architectural interpretations.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about dilated convolutions reducing computational cost
comments.quote: *Chen et al. (2017)* and *(Yu and Koltun, 2015)* used dilated convolutions to increase the recpetive field while maintaining the number of parameters.
comments.explanation: While it is true that dilated convolutions preserve model size, they do not reduce computational cost—on the contrary, larger dilation increases the effective receptive field without downsampling, which can lead to higher activation maps and more expensive operations in subsequent layers. The paper later acknowledges increased computational complexity with atrous convolutions, contradicting the implication here that dilation is computationally neutral. The sentence should be revised to clarify that while parameters are preserved, FLOPs may still increase due to larger feature responses.

comments.title: Duplicate SFNet entries in CamVid table
comments.explanation: Table 2 contains duplicated rows for SFNet with identical values: two entries each for DF2 and ResNet-18 backbones. This redundancy suggests a data entry error or formatting issue. While the values may be correct, the repetition misleads readers into thinking there are four distinct variants when only two exist. It also raises concerns about the integrity of the table. The duplicate rows should be removed to ensure clarity and accuracy.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper presents AASeg, a real-time semantic segmentation network using spatial and channel attention modules along with multi-scale context. While the reported results (74.4% mIoU at 202.7 FPS on Cityscapes) appear competitive, the paper suffers from several critical issues including inconsistent experimental reporting, unclear architectural contributions, insufficient ablation studies, and questionable performance claims.

The paper attempts to address the important real-time semantic segmentation problem by combining attention mechanisms with multi-scale context features. The reported speed-accuracy trade-off appears favorable compared to some state-of-the-art methods. However, the experimental evaluation contains inconsistencies, the architectural novelty is not clearly differentiated from existing work, and critical ablation studies are missing.

Overview issues:

- Inconsistent FPS Reporting Across Datasets: The paper reports dramatically different FPS values for the same network architecture across different datasets. In Table 1, AASeg achieves 202.7 FPS on Cityscapes at 512×1024 resolution, but in Table 2, it only achieves 188.7 FPS on CamVid at 720×960 resolution. Given that CamVid has lower resolution (720×960 vs 512×1024), we would expect equal or better FPS performance. This discrepancy suggests potential issues with the measurement methodology or that the FPS values were not properly standardized. The paper needs to clarify whether FPS was measured on the same hardware and under identical conditions for all experiments.

- Missing Critical Ablation Study for Attention Modules: The paper claims that spatial and channel attention modules enhance features "without adding extra computational cost," but provides no ablation study to validate this claim. Tables 4-6 only explore upsampling methods, kernel sizes, and dilation rates. There is no experiment showing the contribution of each attention module individually or their combined effect. A proper ablation should include: baseline (no attention), SA only, CA only, SA+CA only, and full AASeg (SA+CA+MSC). Without this, it's impossible to assess whether the attention mechanisms actually improve performance or if the gains come primarily from the multi-scale context module.

- Unclear Architectural Novelty and Relationship to Prior Work: The paper claims to propose a "novel Attention Aware Network" but the individual components (spatial attention, channel attention, multi-scale context) are well-established techniques. The spatial attention module (Equation 2) and channel attention module (Equation 3) appear nearly identical to standard implementations from the literature. The paper fails to clearly articulate what makes their specific combination or implementation novel compared to existing methods like BiSeNet, SFNet, or other attention-based real-time segmentation networks. The aggregation in Equation 5 simply concatenates features from three parallel branches, which is a common fusion strategy.

- Discrepancy Between Validation and Test Results: Table 1 reports 74.8% mIoU on Cityscapes validation but only 74.4% on test. More concerning, the ablation study in Table 6 shows much higher mIoU values (79.2% to 80.2%) for the same network on validation. This 5% discrepancy between the main results table and the ablation study is substantial and unexplained. The paper should clarify whether different training protocols were used, if there was data leakage in the ablation experiments, or if there's an error in reporting. Such inconsistencies undermine confidence in the reported results.

- Insufficient Computational Complexity Analysis: For a real-time segmentation paper, the computational analysis is inadequate. While FPS is reported, GFLOPs are only provided for ADE20K in Table 3. The paper claims the attention modules add "no extra computational cost" but provides no FLOPs comparison to support this. A proper analysis should include: (1) GFLOPs for each component (SA, CA, MSC), (2) parameter count breakdown, (3) memory usage during inference, and (4) FPS measurements on standardized hardware. Without this, it's impossible to verify whether the claimed efficiency gains are real or just artifacts of specific hardware configurations.

- Questionable Loss Function Formulation: Equation 8 for cross-entropy loss appears incorrect for multi-class semantic segmentation. It uses binary cross-entropy formulation with y_n and (1-y_n) terms, which is typically used for binary classification. For multi-class segmentation with C classes, the standard cross-entropy should be -Σ(y_n^c * log(ŷ_n^c)) across all classes. Additionally, the class attention loss in Equation 11 is not well-motivated - it's unclear how the class attention map a_ijk is computed or why this loss term is necessary. The paper provides no ablation showing the impact of the auxiliary and class attention losses on final performance.

- Missing computational complexity breakdown: The paper claims the attention modules add 'no extra computational cost' but provides no systematic analysis to support this. For a real-time segmentation paper, readers need a clear breakdown of computational costs. The paper should include GFLOPs and parameter counts for each component (SA, CA, MSC modules) separately, not just the total network. This would demonstrate whether the attention mechanisms are truly efficient or if the gains come at the expense of computational budget. Such analysis is standard in real-time segmentation papers and essential for evaluating the practical trade-offs.

- No demonstration of attention mechanism effectiveness: The paper presents spatial and channel attention modules but never shows that they actually learn meaningful attention patterns. For an attention-based method, visualizing attention maps on sample images is crucial to demonstrate that the modules focus on relevant objects and regions. The paper should include attention map visualizations for both SA and CA modules on challenging Cityscapes examples (e.g., small objects, class boundaries). Without this evidence, readers cannot verify that the attention mechanisms are working as intended rather than just adding complexity.

- Missing hardware standardization for FPS claims: The paper reports impressive FPS values (202.7 FPS) but provides insufficient details about the hardware setup. For real-time segmentation papers, reproducibility requires specifying: exact GPU model, CPU, memory configuration, batch size during inference, and software framework versions. The paper mentions using PyTorch but doesn't specify whether FPS was measured with batch size 1 or larger, which significantly impacts throughput. Standardizing these measurements is essential for fair comparison with other methods and for practitioners to assess real-world deployability.

- No analysis of failure cases or limitations: The paper only presents successful examples and overall metrics but doesn't analyze where AASeg fails. A complete evaluation should identify specific challenging scenarios where the method underperforms (e.g., night conditions, weather effects, rare object classes). The paper should include failure case analysis with visual examples and discuss potential causes. This helps readers understand the method's limitations and guides future improvements. Without this analysis, the claims of robust performance remain unsubstantiated.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide comprehensive ablation studies for each network component (SA, CA, MSC) to validate their individual contributions

- Standardize and clarify FPS measurement methodology across all datasets and experiments

- Resolve the 5% mIoU discrepancy between main results and ablation study on Cityscapes validation

- Include detailed computational analysis (GFLOPs, parameters, memory) for each component and comparison with baseline methods

- Clarify architectural novelty relative to existing attention-based real-time segmentation methods

comments.title: Incorrect cross-entropy loss formulation
comments.quote:
$L_{ce}=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}[y_{n}\log\hat{y}_{n}+(1-y_{n})\log(1-\hat{y}_{n})]$ (8)

where $N$ denotes the total number of samples, $y_{n}$ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is true, and $1-y_{n}$ denotes the probability that the forward propagation result is false.
comments.explanation:
Equation 8 presents binary cross-entropy loss, which is inconsistent with the multi-class semantic segmentation context. Equations 9-11 clearly involve K categories, requiring multi-class cross-entropy. The binary formulation cannot handle multiple mutually exclusive classes. For semantic segmentation with K classes, the correct formulation is $L_{ce} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{I}(g_n = k)\log(\hat{y}_{n,k})$ where $\hat{y}_{n,k}$ are softmax probabilities.

Rewrite Equation 8 as $L_{ce} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{I}(g_n = k)\log(\hat{y}_{n,k})$ where $\hat{y}_{n,k}$ is the softmax probability for class k at pixel n, and $\mathbb{I}(g_n = k)$ is the indicator function matching the ground truth class.

comments.title: Mathematical notation error in convolution formula
comments.quote:
$W^{n}(x)=W^{n\times n}\odot x+b$ (1)

where $\odot$ represents the convolution operator, $W^{n\times n}$ represents the $n\times n$ convolutional kernel, $x$ represents the input data and $b$ represents the bias vector.
comments.explanation: The symbol $\odot$ is standard for element-wise (Hadamard) product, not convolution. The paper explicitly states $\odot$ represents convolution, which contradicts standard mathematical conventions in deep learning literature. This creates ambiguity about the actual operation being performed. Rewrite Equation (1) as $W^{n}(x) = W^{n\times n} * x + b$ where $*$ denotes the convolution operator, or provide explicit definition: $(W^{n\times n} * x)_{i,j} = \sum_{m=0}^{n-1} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} W^{n\times n}_{m,k} \cdot x_{i+m,j+k}$ to resolve the notation conflict.

comments.title: FPS performance claim inconsistency
comments.quote: Our network performs better than most previous architectures with a 74.4% Mean IOU on Cityscapes test dataset while running at 202.7 FPS.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims 202.7 FPS performance, but this conflicts with the paper's own results in Table 2 where AASeg achieves only 188.7 FPS on CamVid at 720×960 resolution. Since CamVid has lower resolution than Cityscapes (720×960 vs 512×1024), the FPS should be equal or better, not worse. This discrepancy suggests either inconsistent measurement conditions or a reporting error. The abstract should either clarify that the 202.7 FPS figure is specific to Cityscapes under particular conditions, or the conflicting values need to be reconciled.

comments.title: Parameter count inconsistency with dilation rates
comments.quote:
Table 6: Ablation study results on Cityscapes validation set. FPS are estimated for an input image of resolution of $512\times 1024$. Best results are highlighted in bold.

|  Model | mIoU (%) | FPS | Parameters (M)  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  AASeg-baseline | 79.2 | 118.2 | 0.76  |
|  AASeg-w/o dilation | 77.4 | 121.3 | 0.80  |
|  AASeg-(r=1) | 79.8 | 115.5 | 0.79  |
|  AASeg-(r=2) | 80.1 | 102.7 | 0.84  |
|  AASeg-(r=3) | 80.2 | 88.6 | 0.90  |
comments.explanation: There is a logical inconsistency in the parameter counts. AASeg-w/o dilation has MORE parameters (0.80M) than AASeg-baseline (0.76M), which contradicts the claim that dilation increases parameters. Dilation rates should not increase parameter counts since they use the same kernel weights but with wider spacing. If anything, removing dilation should reduce parameters, not increase them. This suggests either the parameter counts are incorrect or there's an unexplained architectural difference between these models.

comments.title: FPS and GFLOPs column swap in Table 5
comments.quote:
Table 5: Ablation study on kernel size k in our network using Cityscapes validation set. Best results are highlighted in bold.

|  Method | mIoU (%) | GFlops  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  k = 1 | 79.2 | 118.2  |
|  k = 3 | 79.4 | 120.8  |
|  k = 5 | 79.5 | 127.5  |
|  k = 7 | 79.5 | 136.1  |
comments.explanation: The GFLOPs values in Table 5 appear to be swapped with FPS values. The values (118.2, 120.8, 127.5, 136.1) are identical to the FPS values reported for AASeg-baseline in Table 6, not GFLOPs. GFLOPs for a 512×1024 semantic segmentation network should be much higher (typically in the thousands), while FPS values around 100-130 are reasonable. This creates inconsistency between the tables and makes the computational analysis unreliable.

comments.title: Missing resolution information in ADE20K table
comments.quote:
Table 3: Performance comparison with results reported on ADE20K validation set. Best results are highlighted in bold.

|  Method | Mean IoU(%) | GFLOPs  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  PSPNet50 | 42.78 | 167.6  |
|  PSPNet101 | 43.29 | 238.4  |
|  SFNet | 42.81 | 75.7  |
|  SFNet | 44.67 | 94.0  |
|  DCANet | 45.49 | -  |
|  AASeg | 46.29 | 80.26  |
comments.explanation: Table 3 lacks resolution information for all methods, which is critical for comparing computational efficiency. GFLOPs values are resolution-dependent, so without knowing the input resolution, readers cannot properly assess whether the 80.26 GFLOPs for AASeg represents an efficiency advantage. The other tables (1 and 2) consistently include resolution columns, so this omission creates an inconsistency in reporting standards across datasets.

comments.title: Inconsistent crop size with Cityscapes resolution
comments.quote: random cropping with crop size of $1024\times 1024$ was done.
comments.explanation: The paper reports training with 1024×1024 crops, but Cityscapes images are 2048×1024 resolution. This cropping would discard half the image width, which is inconsistent with the reported 512×1024 inference resolution in Table 1. Either the crop size should be 1024×512 to match the aspect ratio, or the paper needs to explain how 1024×1024 squares are extracted from rectangular images and how this affects the final results.

comments.title: Undefined class attention map computation
comments.quote:
$\mathcal{L}_{cls}=-\frac{1}{BN}\sum_{i=1}^{B}\sum_{j=1}^{N}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\mathbb{I}(g_{j}^{i}=k)\log(\frac{\exp(a_{j,k}^{i})}{\sum_{m=1}^{K}\exp(a_{j,m}^{i})})$ (11)

where $a_{ijk}$ is the value generated of the class attention map of the $j^{th}$ pixel in the $i^{th}$ sample for the $k^{th}$ class.
comments.explanation:
Equation 11 applies softmax to $a_{j,k}^{i}$ values, but the paper doesn't specify whether these are unnormalized logits or normalized attention weights. If $a_{j,k}^{i}$ are already normalized attention weights (summing to 1), applying softmax is mathematically incorrect. The loss formulation depends critically on this distinction.

Add clarification after Equation 11 stating whether $a_{j,k}^{i}$ are unnormalized attention logits, or modify the loss to use the attention weights directly if they are already normalized.

comments.title: Ambiguous multi-scale context module description
comments.quote:
The feature map after passing through convolutional block is split to three different parts with 1$\times$1 convolution, 3$\times$3 convolution and 5$\times$5 convolution respectively. The individual feature maps are then fused together. The output fused feature map is first convolved with a $1\times 1$ convolution to reduce the number of channels from 2048 to 256. The feature map produced is of size $H\times W\times n_{c}$, where $H,W$ are the height and width of the feature map, and $n_{c}$ denotes the number of channels.

The input feature map is convolved with dilated convolution layers with increasing dilation rates of 3, 6 and 12. The dilated convolution layer input at every stage is formed by concatenating the input feature map with the outputs from previous convolutions. At the final step, the outputs from the three dilated convolutions are concatenated with the input feature
comments.explanation: The section presents two distinct multi-scale context extraction methods without clarifying their relationship. The first paragraph describes a parallel multi-branch architecture (1×1, 3×3, 5×5 convolutions) followed by channel reduction, while the second describes a sequential dilated convolution approach (rates 3, 6, 12) with progressive concatenation. These appear to be alternative implementations rather than complementary components of a single module. Add a clarifying sentence after the first paragraph: 'This parallel multi-branch approach represents one variant of the multi-scale context module. An alternative implementation uses sequential dilated convolutions as described below.'

comments.title: Inconsistent tensor dimension notation
comments.quote: where $F_{l}\in R^{H_{l}W_{l}C_{l}}$ is a $C_{l}$-dimensional feature map.
comments.explanation: The paper uses $\mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$ for input tensors but $\mathbb{R}^{H_{l}W_{l}C_{l}}$ for feature maps, omitting multiplication symbols. This inconsistency could misinterpret $H_{l}W_{l}C_{l}$ as a single subscript rather than three dimensions. Rewrite $F_{l} \in \mathbb{R}^{H_{l}W_{l}C_{l}}$ as $F_{l} \in \mathbb{R}^{H_{l} \times W_{l} \times C_{l}}$ to maintain consistency with the notation used for $X$ and $Y$.

comments.title: Inconsistent feature branch count in concatenation
comments.quote: where $\oplus$ represents the concatenation operator and $x_{1}$, $x_{2}$ and $x_{3}$ represents the features of the two branches.
comments.explanation: The text describes three variables ($x_1$, $x_2$, $x_3$) as representing features of 'two branches,' which is logically inconsistent. Equation 5 shows three terms being concatenated: $(f_{SA}(x_{concat})\otimes x_{concat})$, $(f_{CA}(x_{concat})\otimes x_{concat})$, and $(f_{MSC}(x_{concat})\otimes x_{concat})$, corresponding to spatial attention, channel attention, and multi-scale context branches. Rewrite 'where $\oplus$ represents the concatenation operator and $x_{1}$, $x_{2}$ and $x_{3}$ represents the features of the two branches' as 'where $\oplus$ represents the concatenation operator and $x_{1}$, $x_{2}$ and $x_{3}$ represents the features of the three branches' because Equation 5 clearly shows three parallel branches being concatenated.

comments.title: Undefined tensor multiplication operator
comments.quote: $x_{AASeg}=((f_{SA}(x_{concat})\otimes x_{concat})\oplus(f_{CA}(x_{concat})\otimes x_{concat})\oplus(f_{MSC}(x_{concat})\otimes x_{concat}))$ (5)
comments.explanation: Equation 5 uses the operator $\otimes$ without defining its meaning. In attention mechanisms, this could represent element-wise multiplication, matrix multiplication, or another operation. The lack of definition creates ambiguity in understanding how attention weights are applied to features. Add a definition after Equation 5: 'where $\otimes$ denotes element-wise multiplication between attention weights and feature maps' to clarify the operation.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in auxiliary loss
comments.quote:
$\mathcal{L}_{aux}=-\frac{1}{BN}\sum_{i=1}^{B}\sum_{j=1}^{N}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\mathbb{I}(g_{j}^{i}=k)\log(\frac{\exp(p_{j,k}^{i})}{\sum_{m=1}^{K}\exp(p_{j,m}^{i})})$ (9)

\[ I\left(g_{j}^{i}=k\right)=\left\{\begin{array}[]{ll}1,&g_{j}^{i}=k\\
0,&otherwise\end{array}\right. \] (10)

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

where $B$ is the mini batch size, $N$ is the number of pixels in every batch; $K$ is the number of categories; $p_{ijk}$ is the prediction of the $j^{th}$ pixel in the $i^{th}$ sample for the $k^{th}$ class, $I(g_{ij}=k)$ is a function which is defined in Equation 10.
comments.explanation:
The notation is inconsistent: Equation 9 uses $\mathbb{I}(g_{j}^{i}=k)$ and $p_{j,k}^{i}$, while the text uses $I(g_{ij}=k)$ and $p_{ijk}$. The indicator function notation should match between equation and text, and the subscript notation for predictions should be consistent.

Standardize the notation by using $p_{i,j,k}$ consistently and ensure the indicator function notation matches between the equation and text.

comments.title: Inconsistent lambda notation
comments.quote:
$\mathcal{L}=\lambda_{1}\mathcal{L}_{ce}+\lambda_{2}\mathcal{L}_{cls}+\lambda_{3}\mathcal{L}_{aux}$ (12)

where $\lambda 1$, $\lambda 2$ and $\lambda 3$ are set as 1, 0.5 and 0.5 to balance the loss.
comments.explanation:
Equation 12 uses subscript notation $\lambda_{1}$, $\lambda_{2}$, $\lambda_{3}$, but the explanatory text uses $\lambda 1$, $\lambda 2$, $\lambda 3$ without subscripts. Mathematical notation should be consistent throughout.

Change the text to use consistent subscript notation: 'where $\lambda_{1}$, $\lambda_{2}$ and $\lambda_{3}$ are set as 1, 0.5 and 0.5 to balance the loss.'

comments.title: Mathematical notation error in weight decay
comments.quote: momentum of 0.9 and weight decay of $5e^{-4}$
comments.explanation: The weight decay notation $5e^{-4}$ is mathematically incorrect. This evaluates to 5 × e^(-4) ≈ 0.0916, which is an unusually large weight decay value for deep learning. The standard scientific notation should be $5 \times 10^{-4}$ or simply $5e-4$. This appears to be a LaTeX formatting error where the superscript was incorrectly applied to 'e' instead of the entire term.

comments.title: Grammar error in learning rate description
comments.quote: The, “poly” learning rate policy is used to decay the initial learning rate while training the model to reduce over-fitting.
comments.explanation: There is a grammatical error with an unnecessary comma after 'The'. This should read 'The "poly" learning rate policy is used...' Additionally, the poly learning rate policy typically follows the formula lr = initial_lr × (1 - iter/max_iter)^power, but the power parameter is not specified, which is important for reproducibility.

comments.title: Duplicate SFNet entries in CamVid table
comments.explanation: Table 2 contains duplicate entries for SFNet with identical configurations and results. The DF2 and ResNet-18 variants each appear twice consecutively with the same mIoU and FPS values. This appears to be a copy-paste error that inflates the table size without adding new information. Remove the duplicate rows to maintain table integrity.

comments.title: FLOPs terminology inconsistency
comments.quote: For quantitative evaluation of the performance of our network, mean of class-wise intersection-over-union (mIoU) is used for bench-marking the performance, number of float-point operations (FLOPs) and frames per second (FPS) are used for bench-marking the speed.
comments.explanation: The term 'float-point operations' should be 'floating-point operations'. FLOPs is an acronym for 'floating-point operations per second' when measuring computational throughput, but in deep learning contexts it's commonly used to refer to the total count of floating-point operations (without the time component). The paper should clarify whether they're reporting total operation count or operations per second. Given the context of benchmarking speed alongside FPS, they likely mean total FLOP count as a complexity metric, not FLOPS as a throughput metric. Rewrite 'number of float-point operations (FLOPs)' as 'number of floating-point operations (FLOPs)' to fix the terminology error.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
AASeg proposes a lightweight architecture for real-time semantic segmentation by combining spatial and channel attention with a multi-scale context module. While the reported speed and accuracy metrics are competitive, the lack of rigorous hardware-specific latency analysis and the omission of critical implementation details limit the reproducibility and credibility of the claims.

The paper presents a clear, modular architecture that achieves strong performance on standard benchmarks like Cityscapes. However, the evaluation lacks necessary depth regarding the impact of attention mechanisms on real-world throughput, and the methodology section contains significant gaps in architectural justification.

Overview issues:

- Inconsistent Latency Reporting: The paper reports impressive FPS figures (e.g., 202.7 FPS on Cityscapes) but fails to provide the hardware environment or software stack (e.g., CUDA version, TensorRT, or specific GPU model) used for these measurements. In real-time vision, latency is highly sensitive to the inference engine and hardware backend, making these numbers difficult to verify or compare with existing literature. The authors should provide a detailed table specifying the hardware, software, and batch size used for inference, and ideally, provide a breakdown of latency per module to demonstrate the actual cost of the attention mechanisms.

- Ambiguity in Network Architecture: Section 4.2 states that the network does not use a backbone, yet the architecture description is vague regarding the initial feature extraction layers and the total parameter count. Equation 6 suggests a block-based structure, but the transition between these blocks and the fusion of the three distinct attention branches is not clearly mapped. To resolve this, the authors should provide a comprehensive architectural diagram or table listing the layer types, output shapes, and parameter counts for each module to ensure the model is reproducible.

- Lack of Ablation on Attention Overhead: While the paper claims that spatial and channel attention modules enhance features without adding extra computational cost, this is a strong claim that requires empirical validation. The ablation study in Section 6 focuses on dilation rates and kernel sizes but ignores the specific contribution of the attention modules to the latency-accuracy trade-off. The authors should include an ablation study that compares the model with and without the attention modules to quantify their specific impact on both mIoU and FPS.

- Unclear Justification for Loss Function Weighting: Equation 12 introduces a loss function with hyperparameters lambda_1, lambda_2, and lambda_3 set to 1, 0.5, and 0.5. There is no explanation or sensitivity analysis provided for these choices, which are critical for balancing the auxiliary supervision and class attention loss. The authors should provide evidence or a brief discussion on how these weights were selected and whether the model performance is sensitive to these configurations.

- Lack of empirical validation for 'no computational cost' claim: The authors claim that their Spatial and Channel Attention modules enhance feature representation without increasing computational cost, yet they provide no direct evidence to support this. To substantiate this assertion, the paper should include a comparative table showing the FLOPs and latency of the network with and without these specific modules enabled. Demonstrating that the attention overhead is negligible or offset by other design choices is essential for the paper to deliver on its primary methodological promise.

- Missing latency breakdown on target edge hardware: The paper reports high FPS figures on Cityscapes but fails to specify the hardware environment or inference engine used for these measurements. Given that the target application is real-time deployment on edge devices, the reported FPS is not informative without context. The authors should provide a table detailing the GPU/CPU model, batch size, and software framework (e.g., TensorRT, ONNX Runtime) used, and ideally, show a per-module latency breakdown to prove the network is truly efficient for real-world deployment.

- Underdeveloped sensitivity analysis for loss function weights: Equation 12 introduces a loss function with fixed weights (lambda_1=1, lambda_2=0.5, lambda_3=0.5) without justification or sensitivity analysis. In multi-task or auxiliary-loss architectures, these weights often dictate the convergence behavior and final performance. The paper should include a brief ablation study or a plot showing how mIoU varies as these weights are adjusted, which would clarify whether the chosen configuration is optimal or merely a heuristic.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper shows promise in its performance metrics, but the lack of hardware context for latency claims and the missing architectural details prevent a fair assessment of its contribution to real-time semantic segmentation. A major revision is required to provide technical transparency and rigorous benchmarking.

Revision targets:

- Provide a detailed hardware and software specification table for all reported FPS metrics.

- Include an ablation study that isolates the latency and accuracy impact of the spatial and channel attention modules.

- Clarify the network architecture with a comprehensive table showing layer-by-layer output shapes and parameter counts.

- Justify the choice of loss function hyperparameters through a brief sensitivity analysis or explanation of the tuning process.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of zero computational cost
comments.quote: We use Spatial Attention(SA) and Channel Attention(CA) modules to enhance features of objects without adding extra computational cost.
comments.explanation: The assertion that these attention modules operate without adding computational cost is not supported by the provided methodology. Any operation involving feature map transformation or element-wise multiplication necessarily introduces additional floating-point operations. Please remove this claim or provide a comparative complexity analysis (FLOPs and latency) between the baseline network and the model with these modules enabled to justify the statement.

comments.title: Dimensionality mismatch in spatial attention formulation
comments.quote: f_{SA}(x)=f_{sigmoid}\left(W_{2}\left(f_{ReLU}\left(W_{1}(x)\right)\right)\right)$ (2)
comments.explanation: The definition of the spatial attention map in Equation (2) is mathematically incomplete. Applying two 1x1 convolutions to an input feature map of shape (C, H, W) results in a multi-channel output, whereas a spatial attention map must be a single-channel tensor (1, H, W) to be broadcasted across the original features. Please include the necessary channel-wise aggregation operation (e.g., sum or mean) after the sigmoid function to ensure the output is compatible with the input.

comments.title: Missing broadcast operation in channel attention
comments.quote:
:

$f_{CA}(x)=f_{sigmoid}(W_{2}(f_{ReLU}(W_{1}f_{AvgPool}^{1}(x))))$ (3)
comments.explanation: The output of the defined function f_{CA}(x) is a vector of shape (C, 1, 1), but an attention mechanism intended to re-weight an input feature map of shape (C, H, W) requires a tensor of the same spatial dimensions. Without an explicit broadcast operation, the expression is incomplete. Please define the final output as f_{CA}(x) \odot x, where \odot denotes element-wise multiplication with broadcasting, or explicitly state that the output is broadcasted to (C, H, W) before application.

comments.title: Missing negative sign in binary cross-entropy
comments.quote: _{ce}=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\left[y_{n}\log\hat{y}_{n}+(1-y_{n})\log\left(1-\hat{y}_{n}\right)\right]$ (8)
comments.explanation: The formula for L_{ce} lacks the necessary negative sign. Since y_n is in [0, 1] and the log terms are non-positive, the summation as written results in a non-positive value, which contradicts the standard definition of a loss function intended for minimization. Please add a negative sign to the entire summation to align with standard cross-entropy definitions.

comments.title: Inconsistency between training iterations and learning rate policy
comments.quote: The network is trained for 20K iterations with an initial learning rate value of 0.01. The, “poly” learning rate policy is used to decay the initial learning rate while training the model to reduce over-fitting.
comments.explanation: The 'poly' learning rate policy decays based on the ratio of current iterations to total iterations. With only 20K iterations, this schedule is extremely short for semantic segmentation tasks, which typically require significantly more iterations to converge. If the model is trained for only 20K iterations, the learning rate will decay too rapidly, likely leading to underfitting. Please verify if the total iteration count is correct or if it refers to epochs.

comments.title: Ambiguous dimensionality notation for feature maps
comments.quote: e input image $X$ is converted to a set of feature maps $F_{l}$ where l=1,…,3 from each network stage, where $F_{l}\in R^{H_{l}W_{l}C_{l}}$ is a $C_{l}$-dimensional feature
comments.explanation: The notation R^{H_{l}W_{l}C_{l}} is ambiguous as it suggests a 1D vector space rather than a 3D tensor. Furthermore, describing F_l as a 'C_l-dimensional feature map' is technically incorrect, as C_l refers to the channel depth, while the feature map itself is a tensor of rank 3. Please rewrite the space definition as F_l \in R^{H_l \times W_l \times C_l} and clarify that C_l refers to the number of channels.

comments.title: Imprecise notation for convolution operation
comments.quote:
We express a convolution layer $W^{n}(x)$ as follows:

$\mathrm{W}^{n}(x)=\mathbf{W}^{n\times n}\odot x+\mathbf{b}$ (1)
comments.explanation: The use of the symbol \odot for convolution is non-standard, as this symbol is conventionally reserved for the Hadamard (element-wise) product. Additionally, using W^n(x) to denote the function mapping while using W^{n \times n} for the kernel creates a naming collision. Please rewrite the expression using a standard operator like * to denote convolution and distinguish the function from the kernel.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes AASeg, a real-time semantic segmentation network that combines spatial and channel attention with a multi-scale context module. While the architecture targets the speed-accuracy trade-off, the manuscript contains severe internal contradictions regarding performance metrics, architectural claims, and loss function design. These issues significantly undermine the validity of the reported results and the clarity of the contribution.

The paper addresses a practical problem in computer vision by attempting to balance segmentation accuracy with inference speed. The focus on lightweight attention mechanisms and multi-scale context aggregation is relevant to the field. However, the current presentation is marred by inconsistent experimental reporting and unsubstantiated efficiency claims.

Overview issues:

- Internal Contradiction in Reported Metrics: Table 1 lists AASeg achieving 202.7 FPS at 512×1024 resolution, while Table 6 reports the baseline model running at 118.2 FPS under the exact same resolution. This gap of nearly 100 FPS cannot be explained by the ablation study, which actually shows performance dropping when components are removed. The mIoU values also clash: Table 4 cites 79.2% for the bilinear upsampling variant without stating the resolution, whereas Table 1 reports 74.8% on the validation set. Readers cannot trust the speed-accuracy trade-off claims when the internal numbers contradict each other. The authors must reconcile these tables by re-running all ablation experiments at a fixed resolution and batch size, then updating the manuscript to reflect a single, consistent experimental setup.

- Architectural Inconsistency Regarding Backbone Usage: The introduction and methodology sections repeatedly emphasize that AASeg does not use a backbone network, contrasting it with prior work. Section 4.3 immediately undermines this claim by describing the MSC module as reducing channels from 2048 down to 256. Standard backbones like ResNet-101 produce 2048-channel features, but a custom lightweight stem would yield far fewer channels. This contradiction leaves the actual receptive field, parameter count, and computational graph ambiguous. If the model relies on a hidden backbone, the no backbone selling point collapses. If it truly processes raw pixels through a shallow stem, the 2048-channel figure is a typo that needs correction. The authors should explicitly define the initial feature extraction stage and align the channel dimensions throughout the text.

- Theoretical Flaw in the Class Attention Loss: Equation 11 defines a class attention loss that applies standard cross-entropy to the attention map outputs $a_{j,k}^i$. Attention mechanisms generate continuous weights between 0 and 1 to recalibrate features, not discrete class probabilities. Forcing these weights through a softmax and cross-entropy objective misaligns the optimization goal with the module's actual function. This design choice likely introduces gradient noise that destabilizes training or forces the attention heads to behave like secondary classifiers. The paper should either remove $L_{cls}$ entirely, justify it with a clear derivation, or replace it with a regularization term that encourages sparse or distinct attention patterns without conflating it with pixel-wise classification.

- Unsubstantiated Efficiency Claims and Missing Hardware Details: The conclusion asserts that the SA and CA modules enhance features without adding extra computational cost. The ablation tables directly refute this, showing parameter counts and GFLOPs rising as kernel sizes and dilation rates increase. More importantly, the FPS column in Tables 1 and 2 lacks critical implementation details. Inference speed depends heavily on the GPU architecture, batch size, input data type (FP32 vs FP16), and whether TensorRT or ONNX optimization was applied. Without specifying the test hardware and software stack, the 202.7 FPS figure is meaningless for reproducibility or fair comparison. The authors need to report the exact hardware configuration, inference batch size, and framework optimization level, and then revise the conclusion to accurately reflect the computational overhead introduced by the attention blocks.

- Incomplete Ablation of Core Architectural Components: The ablation study in Section 6 focuses on upsampling methods, kernel sizes, and dilation rates, yet completely skips isolating the contribution of the Spatial Attention, Channel Attention, and MSC modules themselves. Since these modules form the core novelty of AASeg, their individual and combined impact on both accuracy and latency must be quantified. The current analysis leaves readers wondering whether the performance gains stem from the attention mechanisms or simply from the specific dilation rates and upsampling choice. A proper ablation should compare a baseline network against versions with only SA, only CA, only MSC, and the full combination, reporting both mIoU and FPS for each configuration.

- Missing visualization and analysis of attention maps: The paper introduces Spatial Attention and Channel Attention modules as core novelties, yet it never visualizes or analyzes the attention maps they produce. In attention-driven segmentation literature, readers expect to see heatmaps demonstrating that SA actually highlights object boundaries and that CA selectively amplifies semantically relevant channels. Without these visualizations, the modules function as unverified black boxes. Readers cannot assess whether the attention mechanisms genuinely improve feature discrimination or merely add parameters. A straightforward visualization of the spatial and channel weights on a subset of Cityscapes validation images would bridge this gap and validate the architectural design.

- Missing ablation of loss function hyperparameters: The final objective combines cross-entropy, auxiliary supervision, and a class attention loss using fixed coefficients of 1, 0.5, and 0.5. The manuscript provides no sensitivity analysis or justification for these specific weights. Training deep segmentation networks with multiple loss terms often requires careful balancing to prevent gradient dominance or oscillation. The authors should report how varying these coefficients impacts training convergence and final mIoU. A simple ablation table tracking performance across different weight combinations would demonstrate that the chosen configuration is robust rather than arbitrarily selected.

- Missing small-object analysis and deployment readiness metrics: The introduction explicitly positions AASeg for autonomous vehicles and robotics, yet the evaluation relies solely on aggregate mIoU and FPS. Real-time segmentation pipelines are notoriously sensitive to small object detection because low-resolution feature maps discard fine-grained details. Aggregate metrics hide critical weaknesses in boundary precision and small object recall. Reporting per-class IoU would expose whether the attention modules actually help the network distinguish cluttered foreground objects. Additionally, listing the parameter count in megabytes and testing INT8 quantization would ground the FPS claims in practical deployment reality.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper presents a segmentation architecture with promising speed claims, but the manuscript suffers from severe internal contradictions in its performance metrics and architectural description. The reported FPS values clash between tables, the no backbone claim conflicts with explicit channel dimensions, and the loss function applies cross-entropy to attention weights without justification. These issues undermine the reliability of the results and the clarity of the contribution. The authors must thoroughly re-run experiments under a unified protocol, correct the architectural descriptions, and provide a complete ablation of the proposed modules before the work can be considered for publication.

Revision targets:

- Reconcile all performance tables by re-running experiments at a single resolution and batch size, ensuring mIoU and FPS values are consistent across the manuscript.

- Clarify the initial feature extraction stage to resolve the contradiction between the no backbone claim and the 2048-channel MSC module description.

- Remove or theoretically justify the class attention loss $L_{cls}$, and replace it with a standard segmentation loss or a properly derived attention regularization term.

- Report exact hardware specifications, inference batch size, and software optimization settings for all FPS measurements, and revise the conclusion to accurately reflect computational overhead.

- Add a complete ablation study isolating the individual and combined contributions of the SA, CA, and MSC modules on both accuracy and latency.

comments.title: Data Error in Table 5 Computational Cost
comments.explanation: The value 118.2 in the GFLOPs column for k=1 matches the FPS reported for the baseline model in Table 6 exactly. Real-time segmentation networks rarely exceed 100 GFLOPs for a single kernel ablation, and this exact numerical overlap points to a copy-paste error. You need to recalculate the actual GFLOPs for Table 5 or correct the header to reflect FPS.

comments.title: Parameter Count Anomaly in Table 6
comments.quote:
|  Model | mIoU (%) | FPS | Parameters (M)  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  AASeg-baseline | 79.2 | 118.2 | 0.76  |
|  AASeg-w/o dilation | 77.4 | 121.3 | 0.80  |
comments.explanation: Removing the dilation mechanism should logically reduce or maintain the parameter count, yet Table 6 shows the w/o dilation variant has 0.80M parameters compared to 0.76M for the baseline. This counterintuitive result contradicts standard architectural expectations and suggests a data entry error. Please re-calculate and report the correct parameter counts for these ablation variants.

comments.title: Invalid Dilation Rate Notation
comments.quote: We use increasing sequence of dilation rates in our network, which is 0, 1, 2, 3.
comments.explanation: A dilation rate of 0 is mathematically undefined for dilated convolutions, as the rate parameter dictates the spacing between kernel elements and must be a positive integer. The authors likely mean standard convolutions alongside dilated ones, or they are mislabeling the base rate. Replace 0 with 1 for standard convolutions and explicitly define how each rate variant modifies the full architecture.

comments.title: Contradictory Efficiency Claims in Conclusion
comments.quote: We use Spatial Attention(SA) and Channel Attention(CA) modules to enhance features of objects without adding extra computational cost.
comments.explanation: The conclusion asserts that the proposed attention modules operate without adding extra computational cost. This claim directly conflicts with the empirical results reported in the ablation studies, which show that parameter counts and GFLOPs increase as the attention module configurations change. Attention mechanisms inherently introduce additional operations, such as channel-wise pooling and matrix multiplications. Revise the sentence to accurately reflect the measured overhead, for example by stating that the modules are designed to be lightweight or by citing the specific parameter increase observed in the ablation study.

comments.title: Missing Hardware and Implementation Details for FPS
comments.quote: Our network performs better than most previous architectures with a 74.4% Mean IOU on Cityscapes test dataset while running at 202.7 FPS.
comments.explanation: The FPS column in Tables 1 and 2 lacks critical implementation details. Inference speed depends heavily on the GPU architecture, batch size, input data type (FP32 vs FP16), and whether TensorRT or ONNX optimization was applied. Without specifying the test hardware and software stack, the 202.7 FPS figure is meaningless for reproducibility or fair comparison. Report the exact hardware configuration, inference batch size, and framework optimization level.

comments.title: Architectural Inconsistency Regarding Backbone Usage
comments.quote: Our network dosen’t use any backbone to extract features from the input image unlike many previous architectures.
comments.explanation: The introduction emphasizes that AASeg does not use a backbone network, yet Section 4.3 describes the MSC module as reducing channels from 2048 down to 256. Standard backbones like ResNet-101 produce 2048-channel features, but a custom lightweight stem would yield far fewer channels. This contradiction leaves the actual receptive field and computational graph ambiguous. Explicitly define the initial feature extraction stage and align the channel dimensions throughout the text.

comments.title: Literature Review Contains Factual Misattributions
comments.quote: *(Mehta et al., 2018)*, *(Paszke et al., 2016)* and *(Poudel et al., 2019)* proposed unique approaches to tackle real time semantic segmentation problem.
comments.explanation: Several citations in the related work section are factually incorrect. Paszke et al. (2016) introduced the PyTorch framework, not a segmentation algorithm. SegNet uses max-pooling indices for upsampling, not skip connections. SqueezeNet (Iandola et al., 2016) reduces parameters through fire modules, not dynamic channel selection. Grouping these works misleads readers about the literature baseline. Replace these references with accurate citations or correct the architectural descriptions to match the original publications.

comments.title: Undefined Operators and Inconsistent Equations
comments.quote:
concat}=x_{1}\oplus x_{2}\oplus x_{3}$ (4)

where $\oplus$ represents the concatenation operator and $x_{1}$, $x_{2}$ and $x_{3}$ represents the features of the two branches. The
comments.explanation: Equation 4 lists three terms to be concatenated but the text claims they represent features from "the two branches." This is a direct numerical contradiction. Furthermore, Equation 5 uses the operator ${times$ without definition and concatenates the outputs of three attention modules. If each attention module produces a spatial mask, concatenating them triples the channel dimension without a subsequent projection layer. Clarify the branch count, define ${times$ as element-wise multiplication, and specify how the concatenated attention outputs are fused or projected.

comments.title: Missing Formal Definition for MSC Module
comments.quote: The fused spatial-channel attention along with Multi Scale Context(MSC) module enables network to extract discriminative and robust features of targets or background.
comments.explanation: The formal results section contains no mention of a Multi Scale Context (MSC) module, nor does it describe how spatial-channel attention is fused. The claim that these components enable the extraction of "discriminative and robust features" is a qualitative assertion that requires a formal mathematical definition. Add a formal definition of the MSC module and its fusion mechanism after the convolution formula to address this gap.

comments.title: Unusually Low Iteration Count for Training
comments.quote: The network is trained for 20K iterations with an initial learning rate value of 0.01.
comments.explanation: Training for only 20K iterations with a batch size of 16 processes approximately 320K samples. On the Cityscapes dataset (2975 training images), this corresponds to roughly 108 epochs, which is significantly lower than the 150K–400K iterations standard in semantic segmentation literature. If the authors are using a subset or a different dataset, this should be explicitly stated. Otherwise, the low iteration count may under-train the network and weaken the reported performance claims. Specify the dataset size or justify the 20K iteration limit.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes AASeg, a real-time semantic segmentation network with spatial/channel attention and multi-scale context modules. While the topic is relevant and the reported results are competitive, the paper suffers from significant issues in novelty, experimental rigor, and clarity that undermine its contribution.

The paper tackles an important problem—real-time semantic segmentation—and reports competitive speed-accuracy trade-offs on Cityscapes, CamVid, and ADE20K. However, the proposed attention and multi-scale modules are minor variations of existing methods (SENet, CBAM, DANet) without clear novelty. The experimental evaluation lacks ablation studies isolating each module's contribution, error bars, and runtime analysis on standard hardware, making the claims of real-time performance and superiority unsubstantiated. The writing and presentation are also below the standards of a top venue.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient novelty in attention and multi-scale modules: The spatial attention (SA) and channel attention (CA) modules described in Sections 4.4 and 4.5 are nearly identical to those in SENet (Hu et al., 2018) and CBAM (Woo et al., 2018), with only minor differences (e.g., using 1x1 convolutions instead of fully connected layers). The Multi-Scale Context (MSC) module in Section 4.3 is a straightforward combination of parallel convolutions and dilated convolutions, similar to ASPP (Chen et al., 2017) and ESP (Mehta et al., 2018). The paper does not articulate what is new or why these particular design choices are beneficial compared to existing alternatives. To establish novelty, the authors should explicitly compare their modules with SENet, CBAM, and DANet in an ablation study and discuss the design rationale.

- Missing ablation studies to isolate module contributions: The ablation study in Section 6 only examines upsampling methods, kernel sizes, and dilation rates. It does not ablate the core contributions: the SA module, CA module, and MSC module individually. Without such ablations, it is impossible to know whether each module actually improves accuracy or speed, or whether the gains come from other design choices (e.g., the specific architecture or training details). The paper claims these modules are beneficial, but the evidence is lacking. The authors should provide an ablation where each module is removed or replaced with a baseline (e.g., no attention, only SA, only CA, only MSC) and report mIoU, FPS, and parameter count for each variant.

- No error bars or statistical significance for reported metrics: All reported mIoU and FPS numbers are given as single point estimates without standard deviations, confidence intervals, or multiple-run averages. Given the inherent variability in deep learning training (due to random initialization, data shuffling, etc.), single-run results are not reliable. This is especially problematic when comparing with other methods, as small differences (e.g., 74.4% vs 74.5%) may not be statistically significant. The authors should report results averaged over at least 3 runs with standard deviations, and ideally perform statistical tests (e.g., paired t-test) when comparing with baselines.

- Unclear real-time performance claims and missing hardware details: The paper claims real-time performance (202.7 FPS on Cityscapes, 188.7 FPS on CamVid) but does not specify the GPU or hardware used for inference. Real-time segmentation papers typically report FPS on a standard GPU like Titan X, RTX 2080, or RTX 3090 with batch size 1. Without this information, the FPS numbers are not reproducible or comparable. Additionally, the paper does not report FLOPs or parameter counts for the full model (only in ablation tables), making it hard to assess computational efficiency. The authors must specify the exact hardware, software versions, and inference settings (batch size, input resolution, framework) used for FPS measurement.

- Inconsistencies and errors in reported numbers: Several numerical inconsistencies appear: (1) In Table 1, AASeg achieves 74.8% mIoU on Cityscapes val, but the ablation study in Table 6 reports 79.2% mIoU for the baseline—a 4.4% gap that is not explained. (2) The ablation Table 6 lists the baseline as having 118.2 FPS, but Table 1 reports 202.7 FPS for the full model—this discrepancy suggests the ablation uses a different setting or resolution. (3) The paper claims 74.4% mIoU on Cityscapes test, but the ablation baseline achieves 79.2% on val—this is unusual because test set performance is typically lower than val. These inconsistencies erode confidence in the results. The authors should clarify the exact configuration used for each table and ensure consistency.

- Poor writing quality and unclear methodology description: The paper contains numerous grammatical errors, typos, and unclear sentences (e.g., 'dosen’t', 'recpetive', 'trim a a lot'). More critically, the methodology sections are vague: the network architecture description in Section 4.2 does not specify the number of layers, channel dimensions, or the exact flow of feature maps through the modules. Figure 4 is referenced but not described in text. The loss function in Equation 8 is incorrectly written as binary cross-entropy (with y_n and 1-y_n) instead of multi-class cross-entropy. The auxiliary loss and class attention loss are not clearly motivated. The paper needs a thorough rewrite for clarity and precision.

- Limited evaluation on small objects and failure cases: The paper does not provide any analysis of failure cases, especially for small objects or boundary regions—a known challenge in real-time segmentation. The qualitative results in Figure 5 show only three examples, all from Cityscapes, and no failure cases. The paper also does not report per-class IoU, which would reveal whether the model struggles with certain categories (e.g., traffic lights, poles). The authors should include per-class results, discuss failure modes, and show qualitative examples where the model performs poorly.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add a comprehensive ablation study that isolates the contribution of each proposed module (SA, CA, MSC) to both accuracy and speed, comparing with standard baselines like SENet and CBAM.

- Report all results with error bars (standard deviation over at least 3 runs) and specify the exact hardware and inference settings used for FPS measurement.

- Resolve the numerical inconsistencies between Tables 1 and 6, and clarify the exact configurations used for each experiment.

- Rewrite the methodology sections to clearly describe the network architecture, including layer counts, channel dimensions, and feature map flow, and correct the loss function equation.

- Include per-class IoU results and a discussion of failure cases, especially for small objects and boundaries, on at least one dataset.

comments.title: Inconsistent FPS between ablation and main results
comments.explanation: The main results in Table 1 report 202.7 FPS for AASeg at 512×1024 resolution. However, the ablation study (Table 6) lists the baseline as having 118.2 FPS. If the baseline is a stripped-down version of AASeg, one would expect the full model to be slower, not faster. This 84.5 FPS gap suggests the ablation and main results use different inference settings (e.g., batch size, framework, or hardware). The authors must clarify the exact configuration for each table and ensure FPS is measured consistently.

comments.title: Missing hardware and inference details for FPS
comments.quote: We present the segmentation accuracy and inference speed of our proposed method on Cityscapes validation and test set in Table 1.
comments.explanation: The paper reports FPS values (202.7, 188.7) but never specifies the GPU model, CPU, memory, framework version, or batch size used for inference. Without this information, the FPS numbers are not reproducible and cannot be compared fairly with other methods. Real-time segmentation papers standardly report the GPU (e.g., Titan X, RTX 2080, RTX 3090) and batch size 1. The authors must add a sentence specifying the exact hardware and software environment for all FPS measurements.

comments.title: Duplicate rows in CamVid comparison table
comments.explanation: Table 2 contains two pairs of identical rows for SFNet (DF2 and ResNet-18). This is almost certainly a copy-paste error. The duplicate rows inflate the table length and may confuse readers about whether these are separate configurations or repeated entries. Remove the duplicate pair (rows 7-8 or 9-10) so that each SFNet variant appears only once.

comments.title: Missing FPS for AASeg on ADE20K
comments.quote:
Table 3: Performance comparison with results reported on ADE20K validation set. Best results are highlighted in bold.

|  Method | Mean IoU(%) | GFLOPs  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  PSPNet50 | 42.78 | 167.6  |
